Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd usually wait until I felt like I absolutely had to do something, then I'd cram in a few hours here and there. With 19 online courses going on, I couldn't really dedicate consistent time to any single one. I'd often study late at night or in random bursts between my extracurricular activities. It wasn't organized at all, but somehow I made it work.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, definitely driven by deadlines rather than any set schedule. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then when assignments were due I'd suddenly be up until 3am getting stuff done. I tried setting regular times early on but with limited resources and a busy schedule, it just never stuck. Deadlines were basically my only real motivator to actually sit down and focus.
Q3: Usually it was panic about an upcoming deadline that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a new assignment or a discussion post, and that would remind me the course existed. I wasn't the type to just browse around the LMS for fun or to stay ahead. It was always reactive—something needed doing, so I'd finally log in to figure out what.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a habit for me the way it probably should have. I'd go through phases where I'd log in a few times in one week, then nothing for a while. The closest it got to routine was right before assignment deadlines when I'd be on there daily for a few days, but that's not really a healthy routine, is it?
Q5: The video lectures were actually pretty useful when I could sit through them. I'm a hands-on learner, so anything with demonstrations or practical examples clicked better for me. Discussion posts were helpful too because I could see how other students were thinking about problems. The peer interactions kept me somewhat connected even when I wasn't attending classes much.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. With low resources, printing wasn't really an option, and reading long documents on screen just didn't work for my learning style. I'd skim them at best. The optional quizzes and practice exercises I often skipped entirely unless I was desperate before an exam.
Q7: If I could see a direct connection to the assignment or exam, it felt worth my time. Anything that seemed like busy work or padding got pushed aside. I needed to feel like what I was doing would actually help me pass or improve my grade. Practical applications and real examples made things feel worthwhile—abstract theory without context felt like a waste.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist for me. If it wasn't required, it wasn't happening. I had too much going on to spend time on things that wouldn't directly impact my grade. Required activities got done because they had to, but optional ones? I'd tell myself I'd get to them later and never did. The mental calculus was always about what's mandatory.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download lecture slides and videos, then study them on my phone during commutes or when I didn't have internet. I used YouTube tutorials a lot for concepts I didn't understand from the course materials. My friends and I would share notes and explain things to each other in person. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS.
Q10: There were tons of times I'd be working through problems in a notebook or discussing concepts with classmates at the campus café, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd watch downloaded videos multiple times while working out or doing chores. I'd also spend hours thinking through problems in my head while doing other activities—that's how my brain works, but it's invisible.
Q11: All the time. I'd download the week's materials in one session, then work through them offline over several days. My internet at home isn't always reliable, so downloading everything at once made sense. The LMS would just show one quick login, but I'd actually be studying that content for hours afterward. It was more efficient for me that way.
Q12: Around midterms, everything piled up and I just couldn't keep up with the attendance. I had some personal stuff going on with money being tight, and my part-time job hours increased. There were weeks where I felt completely disconnected from the course. The CBT sessions I had actually helped me manage the stress, but engagement still dropped during those rough patches.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about bills, the last thing I wanted to do was log into the LMS and watch another lecture. Competing responsibilities were huge—between my extracurriculars, work, and just life stuff, the course often got pushed to the back burner. Confusion with course material also made me avoid it sometimes because I didn't know where to start.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd be logged in and technically watching a video but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. There were times I'd have the LMS open for an hour but accomplish basically nothing because I was too tired or distracted. The activity was there but the learning wasn't happening.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially readings and discussion posts. I knew they were important but there was always something more urgent or something I'd rather be doing. I'd tell myself I'd catch up on the weekend, then the weekend would come and I'd find other excuses. The anxiety about falling behind was low, so there wasn't enough pressure to push through.
Q16: Deadlines were everything for me. My LMS activity would spike dramatically in the 24-48 hours before something was due. I'd suddenly become super active, watching videos, downloading materials, checking rubrics. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all. They were the only thing that reliably got me to do the work.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it motivated me to keep going with that approach. If feedback was critical, I'd sometimes feel surprised and then try to adjust for the next assignment. But honestly, I didn't always check feedback right away—sometimes I'd see it weeks later. The feedback that came quickly after submissions had more impact on my engagement.
Q18: The structure being mostly asynchronous was both a blessing and a curse. It gave me flexibility which I needed, but it also meant I could easily ignore things. Weekly modules helped somewhat because I knew what I was supposed to be doing each week, even if I didn't always do it on time. More structured deadlines throughout would have kept me more consistently engaged.
Q19: Having everything in one place on the LMS made it easier when I actually logged in. But the sheer volume of content across 19 courses was overwhelming. The interface wasn't always intuitive, and finding specific resources took longer than it should have. Clearer organization and fewer clicks to get to important stuff would have helped a lot.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for information or trying to understand the requirements without actually being able to complete the work. Maybe they're confused or overwhelmed and keep coming back hoping things will click. Or they might be dealing with personal issues that prevent them from following through even when they're trying to engage.
Q21: That's basically me. My attendance was terrible and I didn't log in much, but I still got Merit because I figured out how to learn efficiently outside the system. I completed most assignments and did okay on exams by studying in my own way. The LMS activity doesn't capture all the offline studying, the group discussions, or the practical learning I did independently.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when people log in and what they click on, which might indicate deadline-driven behavior like mine. But they completely miss the quality of engagement—whether someone's actually learning or just going through motions. They miss offline studying, collaborative learning, and the mental processing that happens away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you find yourself thinking about course concepts outside of study time, or when you want to learn more than what's required. For me, engagement happened in bursts—moments of genuine interest surrounded by a lot of just getting by. It's more about the mental investment than the clicks.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I actually learned despite my low activity, or the peer support network I relied on heavily. It wouldn't capture the surprise I felt when I realized I was doing better than expected, or how the CBT sessions helped me manage stress without necessarily changing my login patterns. My resilience and motivation don't show up in clicks and timestamps.
